title: 1.1: Tests that HealObject restores metadata when the metadata file is deleted from a disk in a multi-pool setup
1.2: Tests that HealObject restores part files when part data is empty or truncated
1.3: Tests that HealObject restores part files when part data contains wrong content
1.4: Tests that HealObject returns ObjectNotFound error when too many disks are missing in multi-pool setup
1.5: Tests that HealObject restores metadata when the metadata file is deleted from a single-pool setup
1.6: Tests that HealObject restores metadata when the metadata file contains corrupted/invalid data
1.7: Tests that HealObject returns ObjectNotFound error when metadata quorum is lost
1.8: Tests that HealObject restores a part file when it is deleted from a disk
1.9: Tests that HealObject restores a part file when it contains garbage data
1.10: Tests that HealObject heals correctly when multiple disks have different types of corruption simultaneously
1.11: Tests that HealObject restores object data when entire object folder is deleted from a disk
1.12: Tests that HealObject returns ObjectNotFound error when quorum is lost due to too many disk failures
2.1: Places the test for multi-pool object healing in the cmd package test directory
2.2: Places the test for metadata healing in the cmd package test directory
2.3: Places the test for part healing in the cmd package test directory
2.4: Places the test for basic erasure healing in the cmd package test directory
3.1: All tests use Go standard testing package with *testing.T parameter
3.2: All tests follow the naming pattern Test<FunctionName> or Test<Feature>
3.3: Tests use t.Fatal or t.Fatalf for initial test setup operations (such as bucket creation, multipart upload creation and completion, storage initialization)
3.4: All tests properly clean up resources using defer statements
4.1: All tests initialize erasure storage using the established helper functions
4.2: All tests create multipart objects when testing healing of multipart data
